<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Manpukuji 01.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}}|prefecture={{ill|Uji|WD=Q326956}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.913888888889|N|135.8075|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ingen|WD=Q705266}}; {{ill|Mu'an|WD=Q439339}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Miyako Shichifukujin|WD=Q10560192}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q576318]] Wikidata name: Manpuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Uji, Japan}} {{for|the temple in Masuda|Manpuku-ji (Masuda)}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Manpuku-ji | native_name = 萬福寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = Mampukuji.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Manpuku-ji's Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|51.32|N|135|48|21.83|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Zen|Zen]] | sect = [[:en:Ōbaku|Ōbaku]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{URL|https://www.obakusan.or.jp/}} | founded_by = {{Unbulleted list|[[:en:Ingen Ryūki|Ingen Ryūki]]|[[Mokuan Shōtō]]}} | year_completed = 1661 }}
{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], approximately a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Ōbaku Station|Ōbaku Station]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] school, and named after [[:en:Wanfu Temple|Wanfu Temple]] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''[[:en:Senchadō|Senchadō]]'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of [[:en:Imakita Kosen|Imakita Kosen]] and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by [[:en:Tetsugen Doko|Tetsugen Doko]] and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= [[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|location= [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to [[:en:Guan Yu|Kansei Teikun]] image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|1}}
==References== * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}
==External links== {{Commons-inline}} * [http://www.obakusan.or.jp/ Manpuku-ji] – official page ([https://web.archive.org/web/20120504062053/http://www.obakusan.or.jp/haikan/index.html map]) {{in lang|ja}}

{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], approximately a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Ōbaku Station|Ōbaku Station]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] school, and named after [[:en:Wanfu Temple|Wanfu Temple]] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''[[:en:Senchadō|Senchadō]]'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of [[:en:Imakita Kosen|Imakita Kosen]] and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by [[:en:Tetsugen Doko|Tetsugen Doko]] and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= [[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|location= [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>

{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], approximately a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Ōbaku Station|Ōbaku Station]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] school, and named after [[:en:Wanfu Temple|Wanfu Temple]] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''[[:en:Senchadō|Senchadō]]'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of [[:en:Imakita Kosen|Imakita Kosen]] and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by [[:en:Tetsugen Doko|Tetsugen Doko]] and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= [[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|location= [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to [[:en:Guan Yu|Kansei Teikun]] image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|1}}
==References== * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}
==External links== {{Commons-inline}} * [http://www.obakusan.or.jp/ Manpuku-ji] – official page ([https://web.archive.org/web/20120504062053/http://www.obakusan.or.jp/haikan/index.html map]) {{in lang|ja}}

On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''[[:en:Senchadō|Senchadō]]'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of [[:en:Imakita Kosen|Imakita Kosen]] and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by [[:en:Tetsugen Doko|Tetsugen Doko]] and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= [[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|location= [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to [[:en:Guan Yu|Kansei Teikun]] image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] </gallery>

{{ill|Mu'an|WD=Q439339}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Miyako Shichifukujin|WD=Q10560192}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q576318]] Wikidata name: Manpuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Uji, Japan}} {{for|the temple in Masuda|Manpuku-ji (Masuda)}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Manpuku-ji | native_name = 萬福寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = Mampukuji.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Manpuku-ji's Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|51.32|N|135|48|21.83|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Zen|Zen]] | sect = [[:en:Ōbaku|Ōbaku]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{URL|https://www.obakusan.or.jp/}} | founded_by = {{Unbulleted list|[[:en:Ingen Ryūki|Ingen Ryūki]]|[[Mokuan Shōtō]]}} | year_completed = 1661 }}
{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], approximately a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Ōbaku Station|Ōbaku Station]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] school, and named after [[:en:Wanfu Temple|Wanfu Temple]] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''[[:en:Senchadō|Senchadō]]'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of [[:en:Imakita Kosen|Imakita Kosen]] and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by [[:en:Tetsugen Doko|Tetsugen Doko]] and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= [[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|location= [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>

Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of [[:en:Imakita Kosen|Imakita Kosen]] and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by [[:en:Tetsugen Doko|Tetsugen Doko]] and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= [[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|location= [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to [[:en:Guan Yu|Kansei Teikun]] image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Manpukuji 01.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}}|prefecture={{ill|Uji|WD=Q326956}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.913888888889|N|135.8075|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ingen|WD=Q705266}}; {{ill|Mu'an|WD=Q439339}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Miyako Shichifukujin|WD=Q10560192}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q576318]] Wikidata name: Manpuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Uji, Japan}} {{for|the temple in Masuda|Manpuku-ji (Masuda)}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Manpuku-ji | native_name = 萬福寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = Mampukuji.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Manpuku-ji's Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|51.32|N|135|48|21.83|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Zen|Zen]] | sect = [[:en:Ōbaku|Ōbaku]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{URL|https://www.obakusan.or.jp/}} | founded_by = {{Unbulleted list|[[:en:Ingen Ryūki|Ingen Ryūki]]|[[Mokuan Shōtō]]}} | year_completed = 1661 }}
{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], approximately a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Ōbaku Station|Ōbaku Station]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] school, and named after [[:en:Wanfu Temple|Wanfu Temple]] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''[[:en:Senchadō|Senchadō]]'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of [[:en:Imakita Kosen|Imakita Kosen]] and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by [[:en:Tetsugen Doko|Tetsugen Doko]] and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= [[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|location= [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to [[:en:Guan Yu|Kansei Teikun]] image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|1}}
==References== * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}
==External links== {{Commons-inline}} * [http://www.obakusan.or.jp/ Manpuku-ji] – official page ([https://web.archive.org/web/20120504062053/http://www.obakusan.or.jp/haikan/index.html map]) {{in lang|ja}}

{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} temple located in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, approximately a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōbaku Station|en|Ōbaku Station|WD=Q134395107|lt=Ōbaku Station}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} school, and named after {{ill|Wanfu Temple|en|Wanfu Temple|WD=Q3517501|lt=Wanfu Temple}} in {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}}, China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>

In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''{{ill|Senchadō|en|Senchadō|WD=Q11567715|lt=Senchadō}}'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>

The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>

Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of {{ill|Imakita Kosen|en|Imakita Kosen|WD=Q541565|lt=Imakita Kosen}} and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by {{ill|Tetsugen Doko|en|Tetsugen Doko|WD=Q435294|lt=Tetsugen Doko}} and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= {{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}}|location= {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to {{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Kansei Teikun}} image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} *{{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]

Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Manpukuji 01.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}}|prefecture={{ill|Uji|WD=Q326956}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.913888888889|N|135.8075|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ingen|WD=Q705266}}; {{ill|Mu'an|WD=Q439339}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Miyako Shichifukujin|WD=Q10560192}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q576318]] Wikidata name: Manpuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Uji, Japan}} {{for|the temple in Masuda|Manpuku-ji (Masuda)}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Manpuku-ji | native_name = 萬福寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = Mampukuji.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Manpuku-ji's Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|51.32|N|135|48|21.83|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} | sect = {{ill|Ōbaku|en|Ōbaku|WD=Q384728|lt=Ōbaku}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{URL|https://www.obakusan.or.jp/}} | founded_by = {{Unbulleted list|[[:en:Ingen Ryūki|Ingen Ryūki]]|[[Mokuan Shōtō]]}} | year_completed = 1661 }}
{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} temple located in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, approximately a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōbaku Station|en|Ōbaku Station|WD=Q134395107|lt=Ōbaku Station}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} school, and named after {{ill|Wanfu Temple|en|Wanfu Temple|WD=Q3517501|lt=Wanfu Temple}} in {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}}, China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''{{ill|Senchadō|en|Senchadō|WD=Q11567715|lt=Senchadō}}'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of {{ill|Imakita Kosen|en|Imakita Kosen|WD=Q541565|lt=Imakita Kosen}} and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by {{ill|Tetsugen Doko|en|Tetsugen Doko|WD=Q435294|lt=Tetsugen Doko}} and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= {{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}}|location= {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to {{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Kansei Teikun}} image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} *{{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|1}}
==References== * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}
==External links== {{Commons-inline}} * [http://www.obakusan.or.jp/ Manpuku-ji] – official page ([https://web.archive.org/web/20120504062053/http://www.obakusan.or.jp/haikan/index.html map]) {{in lang|ja}}

{{ill|Mu'an|WD=Q439339}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Miyako Shichifukujin|WD=Q10560192}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q576318]] Wikidata name: Manpuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Uji, Japan}} {{for|the temple in Masuda|Manpuku-ji (Masuda)}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Manpuku-ji | native_name = 萬福寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = Mampukuji.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Manpuku-ji's Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|51.32|N|135|48|21.83|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} | sect = {{ill|Ōbaku|en|Ōbaku|WD=Q384728|lt=Ōbaku}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{URL|https://www.obakusan.or.jp/}} | founded_by = {{Unbulleted list|[[:en:Ingen Ryūki|Ingen Ryūki]]|[[Mokuan Shōtō]]}} | year_completed = 1661 }}
{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} temple located in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, approximately a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōbaku Station|en|Ōbaku Station|WD=Q134395107|lt=Ōbaku Station}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} school, and named after {{ill|Wanfu Temple|en|Wanfu Temple|WD=Q3517501|lt=Wanfu Temple}} in {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}}, China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''{{ill|Senchadō|en|Senchadō|WD=Q11567715|lt=Senchadō}}'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of {{ill|Imakita Kosen|en|Imakita Kosen|WD=Q541565|lt=Imakita Kosen}} and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by {{ill|Tetsugen Doko|en|Tetsugen Doko|WD=Q435294|lt=Tetsugen Doko}} and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= {{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}}|location= {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to {{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Kansei Teikun}} image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} *{{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|1}}
==References== * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:مانبوكو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Manpuku-ji]][[de:Mampuku-ji]][[en:Manpuku-ji]][[es:Manpuku-ji]][[fr:Manpuku-ji]][[id:Manpuku-ji]][[it:Manpuku-ji]][[ja:萬福寺]][[pt:Mampuku-ji]][[ru:Мампуку-дзи]][[th:มัมปูกุจิ]][[zh:萬福寺 (宇治市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Manpukuji 01.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}}|prefecture={{ill|Uji|WD=Q326956}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.913888888889|N|135.8075|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ingen|WD=Q705266}}; {{ill|Mu'an|WD=Q439339}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Miyako Shichifukujin|WD=Q10560192}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q576318]] Wikidata name: Manpuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Uji, Japan}} {{for|the temple in Masuda|Manpuku-ji (Masuda)}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Manpuku-ji | native_name = 萬福寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = Mampukuji.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Manpuku-ji's Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|34|54|51.32|N|135|48|21.83|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} | sect = {{ill|Ōbaku|en|Ōbaku|WD=Q384728|lt=Ōbaku}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{URL|https://www.obakusan.or.jp/}} | founded_by = {{Unbulleted list|[[:en:Ingen Ryūki|Ingen Ryūki]]|[[Mokuan Shōtō]]}} | year_completed = 1661 }}
{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} temple located in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, approximately a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōbaku Station|en|Ōbaku Station|WD=Q134395107|lt=Ōbaku Station}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} school, and named after {{ill|Wanfu Temple|en|Wanfu Temple|WD=Q3517501|lt=Wanfu Temple}} in {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}}, China. The mountain is likewise named after [[Mount Huangbo]], where the Chinese temple is situated.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wu |first= Jiang|author-link= |date=2015 |title= Leaving for the Rising Sun: Chinese Zen Master Yinyuan and the Authenticity Crisis in Early Modern East Asia |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=26MVDAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|page= 2|isbn= 9780199393121}}</ref>
==History== The temple was founded in 1661 by the Chinese monk [[:en:Yinyuan Longqi|Yinyuan Longqi]] (Ingen), officially opening in 1963.<ref name="zen">{{cite book |last= |first= |author-link= |editor-last1=Winfield |editor-first1=Pamela D. |editor-last2=Heine | editor-first2=Steven |date=June 7, 2017 |title= Zen and Material Culture|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aykmDwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]]|pages= 139–140|isbn= 9780190469313}}</ref>
In 1664, control of the temple passed to [[:en:Muyan|Muyan]], after which many Chinese monks followed as head priests.<ref>{{cite book |last1= Buswell Jr.|first1=Robert E. |last2= Lopez Jr.|first2=Donald S. |author-link= |date=November 24, 2013 |title= The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |page=529 |isbn= 9781400848058}}</ref> Only the fourteenth priest and his successors are Japanese.<ref>{{cite book |last= Hershock|first= Peter D.|author-link= |date= March 14, 2014|title=Public Zen, Personal Zen: A Buddhist Introduction |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=7zsfAwAAQBAJ |location= |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]] |page= 134|isbn= 9781442216143}}</ref>
On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''{{ill|Senchadō|en|Senchadō|WD=Q11567715|lt=Senchadō}}'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
==Art== The temple's main statue is a seated [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]. Sculptures by the Chinese sculptor known as Han Do-sei<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1916 |volume=7 |title= A Representative Magazine of Things Japanese|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0E4k_SosZK8C |magazine=The Japan Magazine |location=[[New York Public Library]] |publisher=Japan Magazine Company |access-date=January 4, 2024}}</ref> and latticed balustrades can also be seen.
Above the gate of the temple is a carving of the Chinese Characters "義一第", (read right-to-left, "The First Principle") carved from the writing of {{ill|Imakita Kosen|en|Imakita Kosen|WD=Q541565|lt=Imakita Kosen}} and said to have been the artist's eighty-fifth attempt before he finally wrote with a mind free from the distraction of a pupil's criticism.<ref name="reps">{{cite book | last = Reps | first = Paul |author2=Nyogen Senzaki | title = Zen Flesh, Zen Bones: A Collection of Zen and Pre-Zen Writings | year = 1998 | url = https://archive.org/details/zenfleshzenbones00paul | url-access = registration | isbn = 0-8048-3186-6 }}</ref>
The temple treasure house contains a complete collection of Buddhist scriptures commissioned by {{ill|Tetsugen Doko|en|Tetsugen Doko|WD=Q435294|lt=Tetsugen Doko}} and completed in 1678, comprising approximately 60,000 printing blocks which are still in use.<ref>{{cite news |last=Konishi|first=Yoshiaki |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Kyoto Zen temple seeks to preserve history a ramen bowl at a time |url= https://www.asahi.com/sp/ajw/articles/14792700|work= {{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}}|location= {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}|access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="gustav">{{cite book |last=Gustav |first=Konang |author-link= |date= 1972|title=A Millennium of Printing in China, Korea and Japan |url= |location= |publisher=[[National Library of Sweden|Royal Library]] |page=10 |isbn=9789170000119}}</ref> The production of the printing blocks was funded by donations collected throughout the country for many years and through many troubles.<ref name="gustav"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> image:ManpukujiBalustrades.jpg|Balustrades, in {{Nihongo||卍崩し|manji-kuzushi|simplified swastika}} style image:Manpukuji2.jpg|Gate image:ManpukujiAltar.jpg|Altar to {{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Kansei Teikun}} image:ManpukujiHotei1.jpg|Statue of {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} *{{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} *[[:en:Egoku Dōmyō|Egoku Dōmyō]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|1}}
==References== * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045&referer=brief_results OCLC 251800045]; see also '' Imprimerie Royale de France,'' {{OCLC|311322353}}
==External links== {{Commons-inline}} * [http://www.obakusan.or.jp/ Manpuku-ji] – official page ([https://web.archive.org/web/20120504062053/http://www.obakusan.or.jp/haikan/index.html map]) {{in lang|ja}}

{{nihongo|'''Manpuku-ji'''|萬福寺|lead=yes}} is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} temple located in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, approximately a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōbaku Station|en|Ōbaku Station|WD=Q134395107|lt=Ōbaku Station}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Bornoff |first= Nicholas|author-link= |date= 2008|title= Japan|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=X1cLvsZ9t8kC |location= |publisher= [[National Geographic]]|page=233 |isbn=}}</ref> It is the head temple of the Japanese [[:en:Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Ōbaku]] {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} school, and named after {{ill|Wanfu Temple|en|Wanfu Temple|WD=Q3517501|lt=Wanfu Temple}} in {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}}, China. On May 21, 1673 (''Enpō 1, 5th day of the 4th month'') Yinyuan (Ingen) died at the temple.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 414.</ref>
The art of ''{{ill|Senchadō|en|Senchadō|WD=Q11567715|lt=Senchadō}}'' is closely tied to the temple due to its founder.<ref>{{cite magazine |last= |first= |date=1989 |title=Volume 5 |url= https://books.google.com/books?id=i7gTAQAAMAAJ |magazine= Intersect|page=25 |location=[[University of Michigan]] |publisher=PHP Institute |access-date=January 2, 2024}}</ref>
==Architecture== The temple structures were constructed in [[:en:Ming Dynasty|Ming China]]'s architectural style.<ref>{{cite book|last=Yanagida|first=Seizan|title=The Record of Linji|year=2009|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824833190|page=115|chapter=Historical Introduction to The Record of Linji}}</ref>
The arrangement of buildings also follows Ming Dynasty architectural style, representing an image of a dragon.
[[File:Wooden plate.jpg|thumb|''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board)]] The temple features an exemplary ''[[:en:Gyoban|Gyoban]]'' (fish board, used to toll the hours).<ref>{{cite book |author=秋山光和 |author-link= |date= 1967|title= 原色日本の美術: 禅寺と石庭 |url= |location=[[University of California]] |publisher= [[Shogakukan|小学館]]|page=236 |isbn=}}</ref>
